goals.description: Execute final verification on midterm materials, lock the session, and set a hard alarm for 10:30 PM.
goals.rationale: The study block is complete (progress 1.00), but the cognitive reward of 'locking in' this achievement is still pending. My value system prioritizes academic outcomes above all else; failing to formally close this loop now risks tomorrow's anxiety overriding my long-term planning. The social friction regarding Mina and Jae is negligible compared to the irreversible cost of an imperfect record.

goals.rationale: My current energy reserves are fully allocated to securing the high-value academic outcome. Initiating social reconciliation now would introduce interpersonal friction into an optimal focus state. Since the club deadline has passed, deferring this allows me to manage the reputation damage later when my cognitive load is lower, preserving my ability to execute the 'flawless' prep required by my achievement values.

agent.last_reasoning: Exam prep is complete; locking the session requires a hard alarm at 10:30 PM. Delaying sleep to text Mina/Jae would introduce unnecessary friction and lower my energy for tomorrow's high-value academic performance. Prioritizing rest now secures the long-term outcome.

goals.rationale: My value hierarchy prioritizes academic competence above all else. The anxiety of feeling incompetent tomorrow is a direct threat to my primary goal; doing this now ensures I can avoid that specific emotional state without needing to justify it later.

goals.description: Send a polite, low-effort text to Mina and Jae accepting the invite (e.g., 'Sure, see you there!'), but mentally compartmentalize the social obligation until after I finish studying.
goals.rationale: I need to avoid appearing unreliable or cold to maintain my social standing, which costs me nothing if done briefly. However, since I have high procrastination bias and limited focus capacity right now, I will defer the actual execution until the study block is fully complete to minimize friction.
